questionText: Which of the following sentences contains a defining relative clause (without commas) ?
options: My brother, who lives in Douala, is a doctor.
Students who study regularly pass the exam.
The new principal, whom we met yesterday, is very strict.
Yaoundé, which is the capital city, is growing fast.
explanation: Une relative déterminante (defining) n'est pas encadrée par des virgules et apporte une information essentielle pour identifier la personne ou la chose dont on parle. Ici, 'who study regularly' est essentiel pour savoir quels étudiants réussissent. Les autres options sont des relatives non-déterminantes (avec virgules) qui ajoutent une information supplémentaire non essentielle.

questionText: Choose the correct sentence. The relative clause should be non-defining.
options: The motorbike that I bought last week is already broken.
The motorbike, which I bought last week, is already broken.
The motorbike which I bought last week is already broken.
The motorbike that I bought last week, is already broken.
explanation: Une relative non-déterminante (non-defining) doit être encadrée par des virgules. On utilise 'which' pour les choses, et on ne peut pas utiliser 'that' dans ce cas. L'option 2 est la seule qui respecte la ponctuation et la grammaire d'une relative non-déterminante.

questionText: In which sentence does 'that' correctly replace 'who' or 'which' in a defining relative clause ?
options: The teacher, that is from Buea, speaks English well.
The book, that I read, was interesting.
The girl that won the prize is my classmate.
My house, that has a blue door, is on the corner.
explanation: 'That' peut remplacer 'who' ou 'which' uniquement dans les relatives déterminantes (sans virgules). L'option 3 est correcte car c'est une relative déterminante ('that won the prize' identifie quelle fille). Les autres options ont des virgules, indiquant des relatives non-déterminantes, où 'that' est interdit.

questionText:
Read the sentence: 'Cameroonian athletes who train hard win international medals.'
What does this sentence imply?
options: All Cameroonian athletes win international medals.
Only the athletes that train hard win medals; others do not.
Cameroonian athletes, and they all train hard, win medals.
Athletes, who are Cameroonian, win medals.
explanation: L'absence de virgules indique une relative déterminante. L'information 'who train hard' est essentielle pour restreindre l'ensemble des athlètes camerounais. La phrase signifie donc que seuls ceux qui s'entraînent dur gagnent des médailles, contrairement aux autres.

questionText:
Consider this sentence: 'The Fon of Bafut, who is very respected, attended the ceremony.'
Why is the relative clause non-defining?
options: Because it uses the pronoun 'who'.
Because the Fon of Bafut is a unique and already identified person.
Because the verb is in the past tense.
Because the sentence is about a ceremony.
explanation: La relative est non-déterminante car le nom qu'elle complète ('The Fon of Bafut') est déjà unique et clairement identifié. Il n'y a qu'un seul Fon de Bafut. L'information 'who is very respected' est donc un ajout non essentiel, d'où les virgules.

questionText:
Combine the two sentences using a non-defining relative clause:
'Mount Cameroon is an active volcano. It is the highest mountain in West Africa.'
options: Mount Cameroon that is the highest mountain in West Africa is an active volcano.
Mount Cameroon, which is the highest mountain in West Africa, is an active volcano.
Mount Cameroon which is the highest mountain in West Africa is an active volcano.
Mount Cameroon, that is the highest mountain in West Africa, is an active volcano.
explanation: Il faut une relative non-déterminante car 'Mount Cameroon' est un nom unique (un seul mont Cameroon). On utilise 'which' pour les choses, et on encadre la relative avec des virgules. L'option 2 est la seule correcte. 'That' ne s'utilise pas dans les relatives non-déterminantes.

questionText: Identify the sentence where the punctuation changes the meaning of the sentence.
options: Students, who cheat, will be punished. / Students who cheat will be punished.
The car, which is red, is mine. / The car which is red is mine.
My mother, who is a nurse, works hard. / My mother who is a nurse works hard.
The book, that is on the table, is new. / The book that is on the table is new.
explanation: La première phrase avec virgules signifie que tous les étudiants (considérés comme des tricheurs) seront punis (information ajoutée). La seconde sans virgules signifie que seuls les étudiants qui trichent seront punis (les autres non). Les virgules changent radicalement le sens. Dans les autres exemples, la version avec virgules est souvent incorrecte ou le sens est quasiment identique.

questionText: Which sentence is incorrect regarding the use of 'whose' in a relative clause?
options: The student whose phone rang was expelled.
My neighbor, whose car was stolen, called the police.
The writer, whose books are famous, will visit Cameroon.
The woman whose she is my teacher lives here.
explanation: 'Whose' est un pronom relatif possessif qui remplace 'his/her/their'. Il est toujours suivi directement d'un nom sans pronom sujet supplémentaire. L'option 4 est incorrecte car 'she' est un pronom sujet redondant. La forme correcte serait 'The woman whose teacher is my mother...'

questionText:
Transform the following sentence into a defining relative clause:
'The village is called Mvog-Mbi. I was born in that village.'
options: The village where I was born is called Mvog-Mbi.
The village, where I was born, is called Mvog-Mbi.
The village that I was born is called Mvog-Mbi.
The village, which I was born, is called Mvog-Mbi.
explanation: On utilise 'where' pour un lieu (village) dans une relative déterminante (sans virgules car on identifie quel village). L'option 1 est correcte. L'option 2 a des virgules inappropriées (relative non-déterminante). L'option 3 oublie la préposition 'in' (on dit 'the village that I was born in').

questionText: Based on the diagram above, which sentence correctly illustrates the 'extra information' type?
options: The laptop which I bought yesterday has a virus.
The laptop that has a virus is mine.
The laptop, which I bought yesterday, has a virus.
The laptop having a virus is expensive.
explanation: Le diagramme montre les relatives non-déterminantes avec virgules ('extra information'). L'option 3 utilise correctement les virgules et 'which' pour ajouter une information supplémentaire non essentielle sur l'ordinateur ('which I bought yesterday'). Les autres options sont des relatives déterminantes (sans virgules) ou des structures différentes.
